title: What is a 1x1 Convolution?
concept: A 1x1 convolution is a filter with no spatial extent — it looks at a single pixel position but reaches across every input channel there, producing a weighted blend of those channels at that spot.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a CNN's feature maps, what does each channel typically represent?

options.text: The response of one specific learned filter, repeated at every position in the image
A completely random slice of the original image with no meaning
A different color of the original photo, like red, green, or blue

exercises.explain: Right — each channel in a feature map is one learned filter's response map. Now let's meet a very strange-looking filter.

cards.title: Why would you ever use a 1x1 filter?
cards.body: Every filter you've studied so far — 3x3, 5x5 — works by comparing a pixel to its neighbors: this pixel vs. the one to its left, above, diagonally across. But a 1x1 filter has no neighbors at all. It's a single square. So what could it possibly compute? A filter with nothing next to it looks completely pointless.

cards.title: What a 1x1 convolution actually is
cards.body: A 1x1 convolution is a kernel that is only 1x1 in spatial size — but it still reaches across every input channel at that single pixel position. If the input has 64 channels, the 1x1 filter has 64 weights, one per channel, all sitting at that one spot.

cards.title: A straw, not a net
cards.body: A 3x3 filter is like a wide net you drag across a pond, scooping up a neighborhood of water at once. A 1x1 filter is like a straw dropped straight down at one point — it doesn't scoop sideways at all, but it can still reach deep, pulling from every layer stacked at that single spot.

cards.title: One pixel, three channels, one number out
cards.body: Say one pixel has 3 channel values: [4, 9, 2]. A 1x1 filter's learned weights are [0.5, 1.0, -0.5]. The output at that same pixel is (4×0.5) + (9×1.0) + (2×-0.5) = 2 + 9 - 1 = 10. One number, computed purely from blending channels — no neighboring pixels involved.

cards.title: It's not a no-op
cards.body: It's tempting to think a 1x1 kernel does basically nothing, since it can't compare pixels to their surroundings. But it still has learned weights and still computes a real weighted sum across every channel at that position — it's a genuine transformation, just one that ignores space entirely.

cards.title: Reach: 3x3 vs. 1x1
cards.body: Both look at every input channel — the difference is spatial reach:

left.text:
Blends a 3x3 pixel
neighborhood × all channels
→ one output number

right.text:
Blends just 1 pixel
(itself) × all channels
→ one output number

exercises.prompt: At a single pixel position, what does a 1x1 convolution filter look at?

options.text: Every input channel at that one pixel position, and nothing else
A 3x3 neighborhood of pixels in just one channel
The whole image at once, across all pixels

exercises.explain: Correct — a 1x1 filter's weights span the full channel depth, but only ever one pixel position.

exercises.prompt: True or false: because a 1x1 kernel covers only one pixel, it cannot mix information from different channels.
exercises.explain: False — a 1x1 kernel has one weight per input channel and combines all of them, it just never looks at neighboring pixels.

exercises.sentence: A 1x1 convolution has no spatial ___, but it still spans the full depth of input channels.
exercises.explain: Right — the 1x1 kernel drops neighbor comparisons entirely while keeping full channel reach.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes what a 1x1 convolution computes?

options.text: A weighted sum of all channel values at one fixed pixel position
The average brightness of a 3x3 block of pixels
A rotation of the image by a small angle

exercises.explain: That's it — a weighted combination of channels at a single spot, with zero spatial neighborhood involved.

exercises.prompt: At one pixel, an input feature map has 4 channels with values [2, 5, -1, 3]. A 1x1 conv filter has learned weights [1, 0, 2, -1]. What is the output value at that pixel?
exercises.explain: Correct: (2×1) + (5×0) + (-1×2) + (3×-1) = 2 + 0 - 2 - 3 = -3. Next: what happens when you stack many of these 1x1 filters together.
